title: Atomistic Mechanisms of Temperature-Dependent Ion Track Formation in Gallium Nitride under Swift Heavy Ion Irradiation
authors: Jiayu Liang, Shaowei He, Wenlong Liao, Tan Shi, Hang Zang, Yonghong Li, Xiaojun Fu, Chuanjian Yao, Chaohui He, Jianan Wei, Huan He
abstract: The radiation tolerance of gallium nitride under extreme conditions is critical for its deployment in next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices, yet the microscopic mechanisms governing swift heavy ion induced damage at elevated temperatures remain poorly understood. Therefore, this study employs a coupled approach including the two-temperature model and molecular dynamics simulations to resolve the entire processes of ion track generation induced by swift heavy ions irradiation across a wide temperature range. A temperature-driven morphological transition of ion tracks, evolving from discontinuous segments to continuous tracks composed of isolated nanobubbles, and ultimately to fully continuous channels is observed. Under lower electronic stopping loss of 430 MeV Kr irradiation, increasing temperature significantly enhances track visibility, enlarges track radii and promotes nanobubble formation. For higher electronic stopping conditions of 1171 MeV Ta irradiation, continuous ion tracks consisting of discontinuous nanobubbles (~1.5 nm radius) emerge already at 300 K, followed by a thermally activated transition into continuous channels with further radial expansion. At the atomic scale, SHI irradiation induces decomposition of wurtzite GaN into Ga clusters and N2 molecules along the ion trajectory, with Ga-rich regions and recrystallized wurtzite phases accumulating near bubble interfaces, while N2 preferentially segregates within bubble cores. Additionally, zincblende nanodomains nucleate around ion tracks and exhibit strong spatial correlation with radiation-induced dislocation networks, particularly screw dislocations, providing potential pathways for leakage current and increased susceptibility to single-event burnout.
summary: This paper investigates how high-energy ions damage GaN crystals at different temperatures using advanced simulations. It finds that increasing temperature causes ion tracks to evolve from broken segments into continuous channels filled with nitrogen bubbles. The process involves the chemical decomposition of GaN into gallium and nitrogen gas. These structural changes, including the formation of new crystal phases and dislocations, are critical because they can lead to electrical leakage and device failure in next-generation electronics.
main_problem: Understanding the atomistic mechanisms of temperature-dependent ion track formation and structural degradation in Gallium Nitride (GaN) under swift heavy ion irradiation.
main_result: The study reveals a temperature-driven transition from discontinuous tracks to continuous channels, driven by the decomposition of GaN into Ga clusters and N2 molecules, and identifies that zincblende nanodomains and screw dislocations create pathways for leakage current.
method: A multi-scale approach combining the Two-Temperature Model (TTM) with Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations.
model_or_system: Wurtzite Gallium Nitride (GaN) subjected to irradiation by 430 MeV Kr and 1171 MeV Ta ions at temperatures ranging from 300 K to 1800 K.
key_observables: Ion track morphology (radius and continuity), radial density profiles, atomic displacement radius, phase fractions (wurtzite vs. zincblende), dislocation density, and spatial distribution of N2 and Ga clusters.
important_parameters: Electronic stopping power (Se), initial simulation temperature (300 K - 1800 K), and ion species (Kr and Ta).
main_assumptions: The melting-quenching process deviates from thermodynamic equilibrium due to extremely high cooling rates.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows cross-sectional schematics and radial density profiles of the ion track evolution; Figure 2 displays final ion track morphology; Figure 3 illustrates the spatial distribution and radial distribution functions of N2 and Ga; Figure 4 quantifies dislocation density and morphology.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of radiation tolerance in GaN, describes the TTM-MD methodology, presents results on temperature-dependent track morphology and atomic-scale decomposition, analyzes defect generation and phase transformations, and concludes with implications for device failure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
